Chapter 5: Reshaping & Joining Datasets

pivot_longer, pivot_wider, joins, and string basics

Learning Objectives

After this chapter, you will be able to:

  • Distinguish between wide and long data formats and explain when each is appropriate
  • Use pivot_longer() to convert wide DHIS2 exports into analysis-ready long format
  • Use pivot_wider() to create summary tables for reports and slide decks
  • Apply joins (left_join, inner_join, anti_join) to merge datasets from different sources
  • Use stringr functions to clean and standardize messy text data (facility names, region labels)

Key Concepts

Wide vs Long Format

Wide format has one row per unit (e.g., one row per facility) with separate columns for each time period (Q1, Q2, Q3, Q4). Long format has one row per observation (e.g., one row per facility-quarter combination) with a single column for the value. Most R analysis and plotting functions expect long format; most reports and spreadsheets use wide format.

Pivot Operations

pivot_longer() converts wide to long — it gathers multiple columns into key-value pairs. pivot_wider() does the reverse — it spreads key-value pairs into separate columns. These are the two most important data reshaping operations in the tidyverse, and you will use them constantly when working with quarterly DHIS2 data.

Joins

Joins combine two tables based on a shared key column (like facility_id or country_code). left_join() keeps all rows from the left table and adds matching data from the right. inner_join() keeps only rows that match in both tables. anti_join() returns rows from the left table that have no match in the right — invaluable for finding non-reporting facilities.

String Cleaning with stringr

Real-world data has inconsistent text: “Addis Ababa” vs “addis ababa” vs “ADDIS ABABA”, trailing spaces, special characters. The stringr package provides str_to_lower(), str_trim(), str_replace(), and str_detect() to standardize text before joining or grouping. Dirty strings cause silent join failures — rows that should match but do not.

anti_join for Data Quality

anti_join(facilities, reports, by = "facility_id") returns all facilities that did not appear in the reports table. This is the fastest way to find non-reporting facilities, unmatched records, or data gaps. In MNH work, the facilities that fail to report are often the ones most in need of support.

Lecture Slides

Open slides full-screen →

R Code Workshop

This workshop covers the two core data wrangling skills that distinguish intermediate R users from beginners: reshaping data between wide and long formats, and joining multiple datasets together.

Pivoting Quarterly Data from Wide to Long

library(tidyverse)

# Simulate quarterly DHIS2 data in wide format
# (This is how data often arrives from health information systems)
dhis2_wide <- tibble(
  facility_id   = c("F001", "F002", "F003", "F004"),
  facility_name = c("Jimma Hospital", "Hawassa HC", "Bahir Dar Hospital", "Mekelle HC"),
  deliveries_q1 = c(320, 85, 410, 120),
  deliveries_q2 = c(345, 90, 395, NA),
  deliveries_q3 = c(310, NA, 420, 115),
  deliveries_q4 = c(330, 88, 435, 125)
)

# Pivot to long format for analysis
dhis2_long <- dhis2_wide |>
  pivot_longer(
    cols = starts_with("deliveries_"),
    names_to = "quarter",
    names_prefix = "deliveries_",
    values_to = "deliveries"
  )

dhis2_long

After pivoting, each facility-quarter combination is its own row. This format is what ggplot2 needs for plotting trends over time, and what group_by(quarter) needs for quarterly summaries. The names_prefix argument strips the “deliveries_” prefix so the quarter column contains just “q1”, “q2”, etc.

Pivoting from Long to Wide for Reports

# Suppose you have a country-level summary in long format
country_summary <- tibble(
  country   = rep(c("Ethiopia", "Tanzania", "Malawi"), each = 2),
  indicator = rep(c("NMR", "MMR"), 3),
  value     = c(28.1, 412, 20.3, 321, 22.4, 349)
)

# Pivot to wide format for a report table
country_summary |>
  pivot_wider(
    names_from = indicator,
    values_from = value
  )

# Pivot quarterly long data back to wide for a spreadsheet export
dhis2_long |>
  pivot_wider(
    names_from = quarter,
    values_from = deliveries,
    names_prefix = "deliveries_"
  )

Use pivot_wider() when you need to create a table for a report or export to Excel. In consulting, your analysis happens in long format (one observation per row), but your deliverables are often in wide format (one country per row with indicators across columns).

Joining Datasets

# Facility master list
facilities <- tibble(
  facility_id   = c("F001", "F002", "F003", "F004", "F005"),
  facility_name = c("Jimma Hospital", "Hawassa HC", "Bahir Dar Hospital",
                     "Mekelle HC", "Adama Hospital"),
  facility_type = c("hospital", "health_center", "hospital",
                     "health_center", "hospital"),
  country       = c("Ethiopia", "Ethiopia", "Ethiopia",
                     "Ethiopia", "Ethiopia")
)

# Monthly reporting data (F005 did not report)
monthly_reports <- tibble(
  facility_id = c("F001", "F002", "F003", "F004"),
  month       = rep("2024-06", 4),
  deliveries  = c(110, 28, 140, 42),
  neo_deaths  = c(3, 1, 4, 2)
)

# left_join: keep ALL facilities, add report data where available
facilities |>
  left_join(monthly_reports, by = "facility_id")

# inner_join: keep only facilities that reported
facilities |>
  inner_join(monthly_reports, by = "facility_id")

# anti_join: find facilities that did NOT report
facilities |>
  anti_join(monthly_reports, by = "facility_id")

The anti_join at the end is the most important for data quality work. It reveals that Adama Hospital (F005) did not submit a report. In a real engagement, this list of non-reporters is what you would flag to the Ministry of Health or the regional health bureau.

Cleaning Strings Before Joining

# Messy facility names from two different data sources
source_a <- tibble(
  name = c("Jimma University Hospital", "hawassa Health Center",
           " Bahir Dar Hospital", "MEKELLE HC"),
  beds = c(450, 30, 380, 25)
)

source_b <- tibble(
  name = c("jimma university hospital", "Hawassa Health Center",
           "Bahir Dar Hospital", "Mekelle HC"),
  nmr = c(12.5, 18.3, 15.1, 22.7)
)

# Standardize names before joining
clean_name <- function(x) {
  x |>
    str_to_lower() |>       # lowercase everything
    str_trim() |>            # remove leading/trailing spaces
    str_squish() |>          # collapse multiple spaces to one
    str_replace_all("[^a-z0-9 ]", "")  # remove special characters
}

source_a_clean <- source_a |> mutate(name_clean = clean_name(name))
source_b_clean <- source_b |> mutate(name_clean = clean_name(name))

# Now the join works
source_a_clean |>
  left_join(source_b_clean, by = "name_clean") |>
  select(name_clean, beds, nmr)

String cleaning is unglamorous but essential. Without the clean_name() function above, “Jimma University Hospital” and “jimma university hospital” would not match, and you would lose data. Build a cleaning function once and reuse it across all your join operations.

Combining Multiple Operations

# Country-level targets
targets <- tibble(
  country = c("Ethiopia", "Tanzania", "Malawi"),
  nmr_target = c(15, 15, 15)
)

# Full pipeline: reshape, join, compute, summarise
dhis2_long |>
  # Join with facility master data
  left_join(facilities |> select(facility_id, facility_type, country),
            by = "facility_id") |>
  # Remove missing quarters
  filter(!is.na(deliveries)) |>
  # Summarise by country
  group_by(country) |>
  summarise(
    total_deliveries = sum(deliveries),
    reporting_quarters = n(),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  # Join with targets
  left_join(targets, by = "country")

This pipeline demonstrates the typical consulting workflow: import from multiple sources, reshape to a common format, join on shared keys, and summarise to the level your analysis requires. Each step is readable and verifiable.

Key Takeaways

Pivot long for analysis, pivot wide for reports. Most R functions expect long format. Most clients expect wide format. Master both directions.

Joins combine data sources; anti_join finds gaps. In MNH work, the facilities missing from your reports are often the ones that need the most attention.

Clean your strings before joining. Inconsistent capitalization, trailing spaces, and special characters cause silent data loss in joins. Build a reusable cleaning function.

Looking Ahead

Next in Chapter 6 — Data Visualization with ggplot2, you will learn to create charts from your transformed data:

  • The grammar of graphics: data + aesthetics + geoms
  • Bar charts, scatter plots, line charts, histograms, and boxplots
  • Mapping variables to color, size, and shape
  • Choosing the right chart type for your data and your audience
